# Proof that the Sorgenfrey Plane Is Separable

**Proposition.** Let $Y = X \times X$ where $X$ is the Sorgenfrey line, with the product topology $\mathfrak{U}$ generated by basic open sets of the form $[a,b) \times [c,d)$. Then $(Y, \mathfrak{U})$ is separable.

*Proof.* Consider $\mathbb{Q} \times \mathbb{Q}$, the set of points in the plane with rational coordinates. This set is countable (product of two countable sets).

We show $\mathbb{Q} \times \mathbb{Q}$ is dense in the Sorgenfrey plane. Let $[a,b) \times [c,d)$ be any nonempty basic open set. Then $a < b$ and $c < d$. Since $\mathbb{Q}$ is dense in $\mathbb{R}$ (in the usual sense), there exist rational numbers $p, q$ with $a \leq p < b$ and $c \leq q < d$. Thus $(p,q) \in ([a,b) \times [c,d)) \cap (\mathbb{Q} \times \mathbb{Q}) \neq \emptyset$.

Since every nonempty open set contains a basic open set, $\mathbb{Q} \times \mathbb{Q}$ intersects every nonempty open set. Hence $(Y, \mathfrak{U})$ is separable. $\square$
